motivation

A base edit’s predicted effect depends on whether it hits the SNP or a neighbor

scroll ↓
Siraj, Castro … Ulirsch & Tewhey · Nature (2026) · DOI 10.1038/s41586-026-10121-6

A saturation-mutagenesis dataset for the regulatory logic of mutations proximal to an anchor SNP

Siraj et al. saturation-mutagenized 128 cis-regulatory elements, each centered on a fine-mapped trait-associated variant (the anchor SNP, or TARV), mutating every base to all three alternatives. That yields a dataset to read the local regulatory logic around causal SNPs — how a mutation’s effect on reporter activity depends on its distance from the anchor — which is what this deck explores.

scroll ↓
Siraj L, Castro RI, Dewey H, … Ulirsch JC, Tewhey R. “Functional dissection of complex and molecular trait variants at single nucleotide resolution.” Nature (2026). bioRxiv 2024.05.05.592437.
background
TARV — trait-associated regulatory variant. A statistically fine-mapped (high-PIP) common variant, linked to a complex trait or molecular (eQTL) trait, sitting in a non-coding cis-regulatory element.
emVar — expression-modulating variant. A TARV whose two alleles drive significantly different MPRA activity (allelic skew): "the SNP has a real effect."
221,412fine-mapped variants MPRA-tested across 5 cell types
↓
12,025emVars — alter cis-regulatory element activity
↓  selected for saturation mutagenesis (Siraj)
136emVars in 128 CREs — every base → all 3 alt bases, ref + alt backgrounds
↓  anchor's own allele is a significant emVar (skew FDR < 0.05)
53emVar anchors — the primary set: mimicry only makes sense when the SNP has a real effect to copy
scroll ↓
1 / 17

Mutations within 5 bp tend to copy the SNP direction

Within ±5 bp, 66% of saturation mutations match the SNP's minor-allele direction (p<10-45), decaying to chance by ~10–15 bp. emVar anchors (red) vs all anchors (grey). n = 53 emVar TARVs.

scroll ↓
2 / 17

The copied effect is strong at the SNP and fades within ~15 bp

Mean aligned skew ≈ 0.7 log₂ (≈1.6× in the SNP's direction) at 1 bp, falling to ~0 by ~12–15 bp.

scroll ↓
3 / 17

Locus by locus, neighbor effects track the SNP (ρ = +0.73)

One point per anchor×cell: mean within-±5 bp neighbor skew vs the anchor's own skew. Spearman ρ=+0.73.

scroll ↓
4 / 17

The mimicry holds for both eQTL and GWAS anchors

Within ±5 bp concordance by disease class: eQTL 67%, GWAS 63%, both 70% (all above the 0.5 chance line).

scroll ↓
5 / 17

Breaking a motif drives the mimicry — creating one does not

Within ±5 bp: motif-breaking anchors 79%, no motif call 68%, motif-creating anchors 49% (= chance, p=0.72).

scroll ↓
6 / 17

Motif-breakers copy a large effect; motif-creators copy none

Mean aligned skew vs distance, per base pair, by motif class. Breaks (red) high and decaying; creates (blue) flat at ~0.

scroll ↓
7 / 17

The stronger the motif disruption, the stronger the mimicry

Per anchor: within-±5 bp aligned skew vs the strength of the motif-score change. Spearman ρ=+0.43 (p=0.047).

scroll ↓
8 / 17

Same result whether polarized to the reference or minor allele

Concordance vs distance is nearly identical whether polarized to the reference/alt allele (A) or the minor/major-allele frame (B).

scroll ↓
9 / 17

Minor-allele polarization changes nothing; only frame-mixing inverts it

Within ±5 bp: A 66% ≈ B 65% (all 53 anchors); the 9 ref-is-minor anchors hold (61% / 59%); only frame-mixed C inverts to 39% (artifact).

scroll ↓
10 / 17

Independent data (Kircher 2019) replicates the local mimicry

Kircher et al. 2019 saturation mutagenesis of 20 disease regulatory elements. Anchors = variants with a significant reporter effect (FDR<0.05). Within ±5 bp, 57% of nearby mutations share the anchor's direction — above chance in 26 of 29 elements (element-level sign test p=1.5×10-5) — decaying to chance by ~10 bp.

scroll ↓
11 / 17

The copied effect decays within ~10 bp in Kircher too

Mean aligned effect (log₂) vs distance in Kircher — large next to the functional site, ~flat beyond ~10 bp.

scroll ↓
12 / 17

It holds across 26 of 29 disease elements

Per-element within-±5 bp concordance: 26 of 29 element assays exceed the 0.5 chance line (element-level sign test p=1.5×10-5; pooled 57%). Treating each element as one independent unit — the pooled binomial over correlated pairs is not a valid test.

scroll ↓
13 / 17

Denser TF-motif sites show stronger flanking mimicry

Per emVar anchor (Siraj): flanking within-±5 bp concordance vs the site's TF-motif density (motif-matched activity-block coverage). Spearman ρ=+0.25 (p=0.04); +0.31 for the aligned effect.

scroll ↓
14 / 17

Flanking concordance rises with TF-motif density

Pooled concordance by density tertile: low 61% → med 67% → high 69%. Denser motif context → more of the neighborhood is functional → stronger mimicry.

scroll ↓
15 / 17

Requiring larger anchor effects raises concordance

Sweeping the minimum anchor |log₂ effect|. In Kircher’s permissive FDR<0.05 anchor set, within-±5 bp concordance rises 57% → 74%; Siraj’s fine-mapped emVar anchors are already strong, so it stays ~65%. Matched on effect size the two datasets converge — the anchor bar, not the biology, explains the earlier gap. (Concordance here is pooled across pairs; the trend, not the absolute value, is the point.)

scroll ↓
datasets surveyed

Siraj is the highest-power saturation-MPRA dataset we have

  • Siraj / Tewhey (Nature 2026) — 128 CREs, ~170,000 substitutions, fine-mapped anchors + TF-motif / activity-block annotations. Power 5 — primary; all three analyses run here.
  • Kircher 2019 / satMutMPRA — 20–21 disease elements, ~45,000 variants. Power 4 — independent replication (26/29 elements).
  • MAPT promoter (Hauser 2026) — one locus (~2 kb), ~6,000 SNVs, human iPSC-neurons. Power 2 — best NEW dataset from a literature fan-out; single-locus replication (next slides).
  • Other new saturation MPRAs (Patwardhan 2012, e2MPRA, Kwasnieski 2012) are power 1–2 (few short loci) or lack human genomic coordinates (HIV atlas).
Verdict: a workflow fan-out across paperclip + the web found no saturation-MPRA dataset matching or exceeding Siraj’s element/substitution count or its packaged motif annotations. Siraj is the highest-power source; the others are orthogonal, independent-context replications.
scroll ↓
16 / 17

A third dataset (MAPT promoter, human neurons) replicates the ±5 bp mimicry

Hauser et al. 2026 saturation mutagenesis of the MAPT promoter (~2 kb, 5,994 SNVs), iPSC-neurons; per-variant effects reprocessed from GEO barcode counts (GSE325256). Within ±5 bp, 56% concordant (p≈4×10-145), decaying toward chance by ~20 bp.

scroll ↓
17 / 17

...with the copied effect concentrated at short range

Mean aligned effect vs distance (log₂). Magnitudes are compressed by the crude count-based reprocessing (vs the paper’s BCalm normalization), but the local peak-and-decay pattern holds — a single 2 kb locus, so it supports the distance analysis, not the cross-element TF-density test.

scroll ↓
summary

The SNP is a readout of a short local regulatory element

  • A fine-mapped regulatory SNP sits in a short ~5–10 bp sensitive zone (TF-motif scale) where nearby single-base changes reproduce its direction.
  • The exact SNP is less special than the local element it perturbs — mimicry decays to chance beyond the motif.
  • Breaking a motif is robust to which nearby base is hit; creating a specific motif is not, so its neighbors don't mimic.
  • Same pattern for eQTL and GWAS anchors, and independent of reference-vs-minor allele polarization.
  • The pattern replicates independently in Kircher et al. 2019 — 26 of 29 disease elements show the same local mimicry.
  • Denser TF-motif sites show stronger flanking mimicry (concordance 61%→69% from low to high density).
  • Concordance rises with anchor effect size in the permissive Kircher set (57%→74%); Siraj’s fine-mapped anchors are already saturated (~65%), so matched on effect the datasets converge.
  • The local mimicry now replicates across three independent saturation-MPRA datasets — Siraj (128 CREs), Kircher (20 elements), and the MAPT promoter in human neurons — and Siraj remains the highest-power such dataset available.
Caveats. Episomal MPRA in K562/HepG2 (not primary T cells); break/create groups are small (10 / 12 emVar TARVs); "no motif call" is a mixed bag. Siraj anchors are liver/blood/brain eQTLs, not our autoimmune CD4⁺ T-cell eQTLs.
Siraj et al., bioRxiv 2024.05.05.592437 → Nature 2026 · analysis: RA_SLE_pilot/analysis/satmut_distance
